items.correct: Output per unit of input (per worker, per hour) — measuring efficiency, not total output. A small efficient firm can be more productive than a large inefficient one.
items.misconception: Productivity is the total amount produced by a business.
items.random_wrong: Productivity is the number of employees a business has.
items.why: Total output and productivity are different. A factory of 100 workers producing 1000 units is less productive than 10 workers producing 200 units. The ratio matters, not the total.

items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: What is operations management concerned with?
items.correct: The design and control of the production process and business operations — converting inputs into outputs efficiently. It includes capacity, quality, supply chain, and process improvement.
items.misconception: Operations management is the same as general management.
items.random_wrong: Operations management is only about manufacturing factories.
items.why: Operations is a specialised function focused on the production process; it applies equally to services (hospitals, airlines). Students treat it as factory-only or as general management, missing the distinctive process-focused discipline.

items.q: Who is associated with PIECE-RATE pay?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Taylor. “Maslow” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Theory Y managers believe staff are.
items.why: It's Self-motivated and creative. “Lazy and avoid work” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A worker on £1.50 piece-rate makes 200 items. What is their pay, in £?
items.why: Exact answer: 300. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
